questions.question: What does the z-index property control?
questions.explanation: Z-index controls the stacking order of positioned elements along the z-axis. Higher values appear closer to the viewer.

questions.question: Which of these is a valid z-index value?
questions.explanation: Z-index accepts auto (default), positive integers, negative integers, and 0.

questions.question: What is required for z-index to work on an element?
options: The element must be a block element
The element must have a position value other than static
The element must have a background color
The element must have a width set
questions.explanation: Z-index only works on positioned elements (relative, absolute, fixed, or sticky).

questions.question: What is a stacking context?
options: A group of elements with the same z-index
A group of elements that share a common parent and are stacked together
The order of elements in the HTML document
A CSS framework for managing layers
questions.explanation: A stacking context is a group of elements with a common parent that are stacked together. Elements within one stacking context can't interleave with elements from another.

questions.question: Which of these properties creates a new stacking context?
questions.explanation: An opacity value less than 1 creates a new stacking context. Other values that do this include transform, filter, and position with z-index.

questions.question: What is the default value of z-index?
questions.explanation: The default value of z-index is auto, which means the element doesn't create a stacking context and has the same stack level as its parent.

questions.question: If two positioned elements have the same z-index, which one appears on top?
options: The one that appears later in the DOM
questions.explanation: When elements have the same z-index, the one that comes later in the DOM appears on top (back-to-front painting order).

questions.question: What happens when an element has z-index: -1?
options: It goes behind its parent's background
questions.explanation: A negative z-index places the element behind its parent's background and borders, but still within the parent's stacking context.

questions.question: Which CSS property can isolate a stacking context?
questions.explanation: The isolation: isolate property creates a new stacking context, ensuring the element's children are stacked independently of the rest of the page.

questions.question: Why might z-index: 999999 not bring an element to the front?
options: It might be in a child stacking context
Z-index doesn't work with position: fixed
questions.explanation: If the element is inside a stacking context with a lower z-index parent, its z-index is limited to that context. The parent's stacking order determines visibility.

questions.question: What is the recommended approach for managing z-index in large projects?
options: Use CSS variables with a logical scale
Use inline styles for all z-index values
questions.explanation: CSS custom properties (variables) with a logical scale (e.g., --z-dropdown: 100, --z-modal: 500) helps maintain organized z-index values across large projects.

questions.question: Which position value does NOT work with z-index?
questions.explanation: Z-index doesn't work with static positioning. The element must be positioned (relative, absolute, fixed, or sticky) for z-index to take effect.

questions.question: What is the correct order of painting within a stacking context (back to front)?
options: Positive z-index â†’ Negative z-index â†’ Normal flow â†’ Floats
Negative z-index â†’ Normal flow â†’ Floats â†’ Inline â†’ Positive z-index
Normal flow â†’ Floats â†’ Inline â†’ Negative z-index â†’ Positive z-index
Floats â†’ Normal flow â†’ Inline â†’ Z-index
questions.explanation: Within a stacking context, elements are painted back-to-front: negative z-index, non-positioned elements, floats, inline elements, and finally positive z-index.

questions.question: How does transform affect stacking context?
options: A transform other than none creates a new stacking context
It only creates a stacking context with z-index
questions.explanation: Applying a CSS transform (other than none) to an element creates a new stacking context, which can affect how z-index works for that element and its children.

questions.question: What happens when an element has position: fixed and z-index is not set?
options: It goes to the very back
questions.explanation: Without a z-index, fixed elements follow normal stacking rules. They appear on top of non-positioned elements but respect the order of other positioned elements.

questions.question: Which DevTools feature helps debug z-index issues?
questions.explanation: Browser DevTools (e.g., Chrome) has a stacking context visualization in the Elements panel that shows which elements create stacking contexts and their order.

questions.question: Can z-index accept decimal values like 2.5?
questions.explanation: Z-index only accepts integer values. Decimal values are invalid and the property will be ignored.

questions.question: What is the purpose of isolation: isolate?
options: To remove an element from the DOM
To create a new stacking context boundary
questions.explanation: isolation: isolate creates a new stacking context, ensuring children of that element are stacked within that context and don't interfere with elements outside it.

questions.question: In a modal implementation, what should the z-index relationship be between overlay and modal?
questions.explanation: The modal content should have a higher z-index than the overlay so it appears on top of the semi-transparent overlay background.

questions.question: Which of the following does NOT create a stacking context?
questions.explanation: display: flex does not create a stacking context on its own. filter, opacity less than 1, and mix-blend-mode all create stacking contexts.
